topic: reflecting on the year — omori sake work is his best yet

turns.content: i've been doing that thing where you look back at your work from the past year and decide if you're growing or just treading water. and honestly? i think this past year was the best work i've ever done
That's a great feeling — being able to look back and genuinely see growth rather than just volume. What's standing out to you as the work that made the difference?

the sake brewery work honestly. the whole project just clicked. the client trusted me, the brief was open, and i got to do the kind of design i actually want to be known for. like elegant, considered, rooted in japanese craft but with a modern edge. it's the first project in a while where i look at the finished work and don't immediately see things i'd change

That last part is really telling — when a designer looks at their own work and doesn't immediately start picking it apart, it means something genuinely landed. That self-critical voice is always running, so when it goes quiet, the work must be strong.

It sounds like the client relationship was a big part of what made it work. Having creative trust and an open brief changes everything — you can actually push the design instead of playing it safe to get through approvals.

What specifically about the project are you most proud of? The visual direction, the craft in the execution, or how it all came together as a system?

all of it honestly but if i had to pick one thing, the label design. i spent so much time on the typography and the paper stock selection and the way the ink interacts with the texture of the label. when you hold the bottle in your hands it just feels right. there's a tactile quality that you can't get from a screen mockup

The fact that you're talking about how the ink interacts with the paper texture — that's the level of craft that separates good design from great design. Most people would stop at "the label looks nice on screen." You went all the way through to the physical experience of holding the bottle.

Paper stock selection for sake labels is especially interesting because you're balancing aesthetics with practical concerns — the label needs to withstand condensation from a chilled bottle, the printing method needs to work with the texture, and the tactile feel has to communicate the quality of what's inside.

Did you get to work with a printer directly on the production, or was that handled on the client's end?

i worked directly with the printer which was amazing. they're this small specialty print shop that does a lot of sake and wine labels and they really know their stuff. they suggested a washi-textured stock that i never would have found on my own and it completely elevated the whole thing. like the design was good but the paper made it beautiful

Working with a specialty printer who understands the medium makes such a difference. That kind of collaboration — where they bring material knowledge you don't have and you bring the design intent — is how the best physical design gets made.

Washi-textured stock for a sake label is perfect. It immediately communicates craft, tradition, and care before you even read a word. And washi has that beautiful irregular fiber texture that catches ink in interesting ways, especially if you're using any embossing or spot techniques.

This sounds like the kind of project that should be a centerpiece in your portfolio. Have you documented it well — process shots, close-up details of the printing, the bottles in context?

i did actually. i photographed the whole thing pretty thoroughly. the printer even let me shoot in their workshop while they were doing the press run which gave me some great process images. honestly the case study almost writes itself because the story is so clear — brief, concept, craft, final product

That's ideal. A case study that has a clean narrative arc — from brief to concept to craft to finished product — is so much more compelling than just showing final mockups. And having press run photography adds an authenticity that you can't fake. It shows potential clients that you care about the production process, not just the Photoshop comp.

This is exactly the kind of work that attracts more of the same. If you want to do more premium packaging, more craft-oriented branding, this project is your proof of concept. Put it front and center.

Do you feel like this project shifted what you want your career to focus on going forward?

yeah it really did. like before this project i was taking whatever came through the door — ui gigs, random branding for whoever, just trying to keep the invoices flowing. which i still need to do obviously, freelancing is freelancing. but now i have a clear picture of what my best work looks like and what kind of clients bring it out of me

That clarity is incredibly valuable. A lot of freelancers go years without figuring out what their "best self" work looks like — they just stay in survival mode, taking everything, never building a coherent body of work.

You now have a proof point: when the brief is open, the client trusts you, and the product has real craft behind it, you do your best work. That's not just a nice feeling — it's a strategy. You can actively seek out more clients in that space. Craft beverages, artisanal food, heritage brands — there's a whole category of clients who value exactly what you're describing.

The hard part is the transition. You still need to keep the lights on with whatever work comes in, but you can start tilting your portfolio, your outreach, and your positioning toward the work you want more of. It doesn't happen overnight, but having that one anchor project makes it possible.

exactly. and i think the key thing i learned this year is that the work gets better when i'm not just executing a brief but actually collaborating with people who care about their product. the brewery owner, the printer, even the paper supplier — everyone was invested. it wasn't just a transaction, it was a shared thing

That's a really mature observation. Design as collaboration rather than service — when everyone in the chain cares about the outcome, the quality compounds at every stage. The brewery owner's pride in their product, the printer's expertise with materials, your design sensibility — each person elevated the others.

That's also a filter for future clients. You can start asking yourself: "Is this person invested in the quality of the outcome, or do they just need a deliverable?" Both can pay the bills, but only one produces work you're proud of.

It sounds like this year was a real turning point. Not just in the quality of the work but in understanding what conditions make that quality possible. That's the kind of growth that compounds year over year.

honestly yeah. i'm going into this year feeling more confident about my work than i have in a long time. still anxious about money obviously, that never goes away when you freelance. but at least i know what i'm aiming for now. that's something

The money anxiety never fully disappears in freelancing — that's just the deal. But having creative confidence and a clear direction is what makes the hustle sustainable. You're not just chasing invoices anymore; you're building toward something.

You had a strong year. The work speaks for itself, and now you have the portfolio piece to prove it. Use that momentum — update the portfolio, lead with the sake project, and let the work attract the clients you actually want to work with.

Here's to more of that this year.
